def: A category that requires collecting everything the game tracks as completion, not just reaching the end.
The 1996 add-on board that brought hardware 3D acceleration to consumer PCs and defined early gaming graphics.
Standard-definition interlaced video, 480 visible lines drawn as two alternating fields per frame.
Standard-definition progressive video, 480 lines drawn whole each frame; the GameCube's Progressive Scan output.
A Texas Instruments Sitara application processor with a quad-core Cortex-A53 cluster and several real-time cores.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: A category that finishes the game as fast as possible by any means, glitches included.
Sony's tensioned vertical-wire mask that separates color phosphors into continuous stripes instead of dots.
Forcing the game to run player-controlled bytes as program code, used to rewrite the game state.
The community project porting Linux to Apple Silicon Macs; source of the Rust drm/asahi GPU driver, founded by Hector Martin.
ArtX, a startup founded by ex-SGI engineers, designed Flipper; ATI bought ArtX in 2000, which is why the console carries an ATI badge.
An open-hardware single-board computer from the BeagleBoard.org Foundation built around the TI AM625 SoC.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: The generic putty-colored tower or desktop case that nearly every PC shipped in during the era.
Android's inter-process communication driver, rewritten in Rust as an early demonstration of a real Rust kernel subsystem.
The process that stripped the Necrontyr of flesh and soul and bound their minds into living-metal bodies, creating the Necrons.
A classic lexical ranking function that scores a passage by how well its rare query words match, normalized for length.
A bayonet locking coaxial connector used for professional video feeds on broadcast monitors.
An in-game schedule tracker that records the routines, deadlines, and sidequests of Termina's residents across the cycle.
topicTitle: The Legend of Zelda - Majora's Mask

def: Equipping a non-sword item onto the B button by selecting it on C-Right and pulling the Master Sword.

term: C'tan, def: "Star-gods of pure energy that fed on stellar power and, given necrodermis bodies by the Necrontyr, became their tyrant overlords."

def: When a network partition occurs, a distributed system must choose between consistency and availability; it cannot keep both during the split.
A read-only optical disc holding about 650 MB, used to distribute software after floppies ran out of room.
An SPI line the controller asserts low to address one specific peripheral on a shared bus.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: YPbPr analog video split into luma (Y) and two color-difference channels, carried on three BNC or RCA lines.
An analog video connection splitting the picture into luma and two color-difference channels (YPbPr), capable of carrying progressive scan.
A single-cable signal carrying luma, chroma, and sync mixed together, the lowest-quality common input.
The problem of getting a set of nodes to agree on a single value even though some may fail.
The current Linux terminology for the old master/slave roles; the controller drives SCLK and CS, the peripheral responds.
Alignment of the three electron beams so red, green, and blue land on the correct phosphors across the screen.
A 64-bit Arm application core designed for power efficiency, used in clusters of up to four on the AM625.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: The angle-based measure of how aligned two embedding vectors are, used to rank passages by meaning.
A console cowboy, a freelance hacker who runs the matrix on a custom cyberspace deck.
Clock Phase; sets whether data is sampled on the leading (0) or trailing (1) clock edge.
Clock Polarity; sets whether SCLK idles low (0) or high (1).
A model that reads a question and a passage together and scores true relevance, sharper than comparing them as separate vectors.
Cathode ray tube; the deep glass display that fired electron beams at colored phosphors behind the glass.
Chip Select, also called Slave Select (SS); an active-low line that activates one peripheral on the shared bus.
A consensual hallucination, a shared graphical data-space that operators enter directly through the nervous system.
A topology that strings peripherals MISO-to-MOSI so one long shift register carries data through all of them under a shared CS.
An extra GPIO on many SPI displays that tells the panel whether incoming bytes are pixel data or a command.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: Rearranging a disk's file fragments into contiguous order so the drive head seeks less.
A data structure that describes hardware to the kernel so drivers can bind without that knowledge being compiled in.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: A fragment that patches the base device tree to add or enable a peripheral without editing the main file.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: A device that carried data as audible tones over an ordinary phone line, topping out near 56 kbit/s.
A second video port on the underside of early GameCubes carrying an uncompressed digital Y'CbCr signal; removed on the later DOL-101 revision.
Direct Memory Access; hardware moves transfer buffers without the CPU copying each word.
Spacing between two same-color phosphor dots on a shadow-mask tube; aperture-grille tubes use stripe pitch instead.
A vector of numbers that places a piece of text in a space where nearby vectors mean similar things.
A weak model where, once writes stop, all replicas converge to the same value after some delay.
The most powerful transformation, an adult swordsman form usable only in boss battles and earned after trading away all other non-transformation masks.
topicTitle: The Legend of Zelda - Majora's Mask

def: The 162 MHz graphics and I/O chip designed by ArtX (acquired by ATI mid-development), with 3 MB of embedded 1T-SRAM as a frame and texture buffer.
An input that must land on one exact frame, roughly 1/60th of a second on N64 hardware, to work.
Both directions transfer at once; one bit goes out on MOSI while one bit comes in on MISO every clock.
The studio that developed the Generation I Pokemon games, founded by Satoshi Tajiri.
Necron arms that strip matter apart molecule by molecule, breaking the bonds of an enemy into energy the Necrons can reuse.
The GameCube's 486 MHz 32-bit PowerPC CPU, built by IBM from the PowerPC 750CXe (G3) core with custom paired-single floating-point extensions.
How square and straight the raster is; service controls correct bowing, tilt, and size.
Looting a chest's contents without the opening animation by triggering an item delay and then taking damage or swimming.
A term coined from the 40k tagline for a bleak, hopeless far-future tone of unending war.
The rule that every claim must trace to a retrieved passage; if nothing relevant is found, the honest answer is silence.
Combining dense vector similarity with lexical keyword matching so both meaning and exact terms are caught.
Intrusion Countermeasures Electronics, the defensive software guarding systems in the matrix; deadly variants are called black ICE.
A property where applying the same operation more than once has the same effect as applying it exactly once.
A single-chip TFT LCD driver for 240 by 320 panels that accepts a 3- or 4-line SPI interface.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: The galaxy-spanning, authoritarian human empire of the 41st Millennium, ruled in name by the entombed God-Emperor.
A state where Link's sword stays permanently active without a swing, used as a setup for many other tricks.
The Song of Time played backwards; it slows the clock to one-third speed on the N64 release and half speed in the 3DS remake.
topicTitle: The Legend of Zelda - Majora's Mask

def: Connecting a human nervous system directly to cyberspace through a deck and trode set.
The region where Generation I is set, home to its 151 Pokemon and eight Gyms.
The kernel crate's Result type whose error is the kernel Error enum, used so fallible setup returns errno-style codes instead of panicking.
A gathering where people hauled their PCs to one location and wired them together for multiplayer games.
A strong consistency model where each operation appears to take effect instantaneously at one point between its call and return, respecting real-time order.
The physical cable joining two Game Boys so players can trade and battle Pokemon.
The Linux Kernel Mailing List, the main forum where patches are posted, reviewed, and argued over in public.
Kernel code (often a driver) that can be inserted into or removed from a running kernel without rebooting, via insmod or modprobe.
A longterm-support kernel chosen for extended maintenance, receiving backported fixes for years rather than months.
The canonical kernel tree maintained by Linus Torvalds, into which all new features ultimately land.
A developer responsible for reviewing and merging changes to a part of the kernel and keeping it working over time.
The roughly two-week period after a release when Linus pulls new features from subsystem maintainers into mainline.
A method to obtain Mew in retail Red and Blue by exploiting the Trainer-Fly glitch without an official event.
The kernel developer who leads the Rust for Linux project and maintains its core infrastructure.
The proprietary 8 cm optical disc the GameCube used, holding about 1.46 GB, manufactured by Matsushita to cut cost and resist copying.
Master In Slave Out; the data line carrying bits from the peripheral back to the controller.
A glitch Pokemon that appears from undefined data and, on capture or encounter, adds 128 to the sixth bag item.
A kernel design where scheduling, memory, filesystems, networking, and drivers all run in one privileged address space.
Master Out Slave In; the data line carrying bits from the controller to the peripheral.
Microsoft's command-line disk operating system that ran most PCs before and beneath Windows 95.
An ancient race of androids in living-metal bodies, once the flesh-and-blood Necrontyr, now slumbering across the galaxy and waking to reconquer it.
The short-lived, radiation-cursed organic species that built a star-empire, lost the War in Heaven, and became the Necrons through biotransference.
The second Tessier-Ashpool AI; a being of personality and memory that builds persistent simulated worlds.
A Rust mode that drops the standard library and builds only on core (and optionally alloc), required because the kernel has no OS underneath it.
A Rust driver for GSP-based NVIDIA GPUs (Turing/RTX 20 and newer), intended successor to Nouveau, led by Danilo Krummrich at Red Hat.
The 525-line, ~60 Hz analog color standard used in North America and Japan.
The normal consumer mode where the image is enlarged past the bezel so the signal's hidden edges fall off-screen.
A landmark Link can activate to warp via the Song of Soaring; in international N64 versions it also doubles as a temporary quicksave point.
topicTitle: The Legend of Zelda - Majora's Mask

def: The 625-line, 50 Hz analog color standard used across much of Europe and elsewhere.
A network failure that splits nodes into groups that cannot communicate with each other.
Lamport's family of consensus algorithms; correct and influential, but famously hard to follow.
The coating that emits light when struck by the electron beam, arranged as stripes on Trinitron tubes.
A macro and crate from Rust for Linux that initializes pinned, self-referential, or oversized structs in place, with a fallible variant for error paths.
The in-game catalog of Pokemon that the player fills by seeing and catching each species.
A display mode that draws every line of each frame in one pass rather than interlacing two fields.
A subset of nodes (usually a majority) whose agreement is required before an action counts as decided.
A leader-based consensus algorithm by Ongaro and Ousterhout, designed to be understandable while matching Paxos in power.
A street mercenary with surgically implanted weapons and combat augmentation, exemplified by Molly Millions.
Necron auto-repair systems that knit a damaged body back together or teleport its mind to a tomb for rebuilding.
A way to merge two ranked lists by rewarding items that rank highly in either, without needing to normalize their scores.
Firmware and hardware checks that tie a console to NTSC-U, NTSC-J, or PAL game discs and refuse others.
A weekly pre-release snapshot, rc1 through about rc7, issued during the bug-fix-only stabilization phase.
Keeping copies of the same data on multiple nodes for durability, availability, or read scaling.
Answering with a language model that is fed passages retrieved from a fixed source, so it speaks from documents rather than memory.
RGB video with horizontal and vertical sync on separate wires, five conductors total, the VGA arrangement.
Steering the game's pseudorandom state to force a desired outcome on demand.
Real-time attack; a run timed by a real clock from start to finish, as opposed to in-game or tool-assisted timing.
A connection that splits luma and chroma onto separate wires for a sharper image than composite.
A Rust API that wraps unsafe C kernel interfaces so callers can use them without writing unsafe code.
Game Freak founder who conceived Pokemon out of childhood insect collecting.
One horizontal pass of the electron beam; visible dark gaps between lines define the CRT look.
Serial Clock; the synchronizing clock generated by the controller for every bit on the bus.
A text terminal exposed over a UART, giving boot logs and a login shell before any network or display works.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: A perforated metal plate behind the screen with one hole per triangular phosphor triad, the older alternative to aperture grille.
A distribution model where a free partial version was freely copied to entice purchase of the full registered version.
Simulated stimulation; a technology that broadcasts one person's full sensory experience into another's nervous system.
A complete computer on one board running a full operating system, distinct from a bare-metal microcontroller.
topicTitle: The BeaglePlay and SPI Display Bring-up

def: The ocarina melody that sends Link back to the dawn of the first day and saves progress on the Nintendo 64 version.
topicTitle: The Legend of Zelda - Majora's Mask

def: Serial Peripheral Interface, a synchronous full-duplex master/slave serial bus introduced by Motorola in the 1980s.
The four CPOL/CPHA combinations, with CPOL as the high bit and CPHA as the low bit; mode 0 and mode 3 are most common.
The Linux kernel structure that is the controller-side proxy for one peripheral on the bus.
A use-after-free exploit where the game keeps writing to a freed actor's old memory, letting runners corrupt whatever loads there next.
A self-contained area of the kernel (filesystems, DRM, networking) with its own maintainers and merge process.
A developer responsible for a portion of the kernel who reviews patches and forwards them upstream toward mainline.
RGB where composite sync rides on the green channel, requiring only three wires (RGsB); among Sony consoles only the PS2 outputs it, and only in 480p-and-up RGB mode.
A system call: the controlled entry point through which user-space programs request services from the kernel.
The parallel world Link enters in Majora's Mask, centered on Clock Town with four regions facing the cardinal directions.
topicTitle: The Legend of Zelda - Majora's Mask

def: Gibson's name for the global cyberspace grid; the abstract representation of all networked data.
Szarekh, last supreme monarch of the Necrons, who sealed the C'tan pact, shattered the star-gods in penance, and exiled himself before returning in the modern era.
The Boston-Atlanta Metropolitan Axis, a continuous domed megacity along the US east coast.
A parallel dimension of psychic energy, also called the Immaterium, that is both the source of Chaos and the only medium for faster-than-light travel.
A repeating 72-hour loop, about 54 minutes of real time, that resets when the moon falls or the Song of Time is played.
topicTitle: The Legend of Zelda - Majora's Mask

def: A planet honeycombed with stasis crypts where a Necron dynasty slept through the Great Sleep, now waking dynasty by dynasty.
A run built with slowdown, save states, and frame-by-frame input, used to find theoretical limits rather than to compete with human runs.
Sony's aperture-grille CRT line introduced in the late 1960s, known for brightness and a flat-vertical screen.
TV Lines, the horizontal resolution the tube itself can resolve, distinct from the signal's scan-line count.
An atomic-commit protocol (prepare then commit) that blocks if the coordinator fails after votes are collected.
A display mode that shrinks the image inward so the full signal and a clean black border are visible.
A Pokemon catchable in only one version of a paired release, pushing players to trade.
The galaxy-spanning war between the Necrontyr-and-C'tan alliance and the Old Ones, fought roughly sixty million years before the 41st Millennium.
The 1995 Microsoft operating system that merged DOS and Windows and introduced the Start menu and taskbar.
One of the two Tessier-Ashpool AIs; a cold, scheming planner that drives the plot toward its own merger.
Loading a scene transition while the game is confused about the destination, sending Link somewhere unintended, sometimes straight to an ending.
